name: Auth Fundamentals
description: Cookies, sessions, passwords, tokens, and web security basics every developer needs.

cards.front: Why does authentication exist? What does it mean that HTTP is stateless?
cards.back:
• HTTP is stateless — every request your browser sends is a complete stranger to the server
• The server has no built-in memory of who you are between requests
• Without auth, anyone could access anyone's data just by visiting the right URL
• Authentication is how a server answers the question: "Who is making this request?"
• Think of it like a hotel front desk — you check in once, get a keycard, and show it each visit
• Authorization is the follow-up question: "Is this person allowed to do this?"
• Every auth system is essentially a workaround for HTTP having no memory
• This is why you need cookies, tokens, or sessions — they give HTTP a form of memory

cards.front: What is a password and why should you never store passwords as plaintext?
cards.back:
• A password is a secret string only the user should know, used to prove their identity
• Plaintext storage means saving the actual password as-is in your database (e.g., "myPassword123")
• If your database is breached, attackers instantly have every user's real password
• People reuse passwords — a leak from your site can compromise their bank, email, and social accounts
• Even your own employees or database admins should never be able to read user passwords
• Major companies like Adobe and LinkedIn suffered massive damage from plaintext or weak storage
• The rule is simple: if you can read a user's password from your database, you are doing it wrong
• Instead, you store a hashed version — a one-way transformation that cannot be reversed

cards.front: What is password hashing? What are salts, and why is bcrypt preferred over plain MD5 or SHA?
cards.back:
• Hashing is a one-way function — it turns "myPassword" into a fixed-length scrambled string
• You cannot reverse a hash back to the original password; you can only hash a guess and compare
• MD5 and SHA are fast hashes — an attacker can try billions of guesses per second with modern GPUs
• bcrypt is intentionally slow, making brute-force attacks take years instead of minutes
• A salt is a random string added to each password before hashing, so identical passwords produce different hashes
• Without salts, attackers use precomputed rainbow tables to look up common password hashes instantly
• bcrypt automatically generates and stores a unique salt for each password
• When a user logs in, you hash their input with the same salt and compare it to the stored hash
• Other good options include Argon2 and scrypt — the key is using a slow, salted algorithm

cards.front: What does being "logged in" actually mean technically?
cards.back:
• Being logged in means the server has a way to recognize your requests without asking for your password again
• At login, you prove your identity once (usually with a password), and the server gives you a token or session ID
• Your browser stores this token and attaches it to every subsequent request automatically
• The server checks this token on each request to know who you are — this is your "logged in" state
• There is no magic "logged in" flag — it is just the presence of a valid, unexpired credential
• If the token expires or is deleted, you are "logged out" even though nothing changed about your account
• Two common approaches: the server remembers you (sessions) or gives you a self-contained proof (JWTs)
• Logging out means destroying that token — on the server side, client side, or both

cards.front: What is a cookie? How do browsers handle cookies?
cards.back:
• A cookie is a small piece of data the server sends to the browser, which the browser stores locally
• The browser automatically attaches matching cookies to every subsequent request to that domain
• The server sets cookies using the Set-Cookie response header, e.g., Set-Cookie: sessionId=abc123
• Cookies have attributes: expiration date, domain scope, path scope, and security flags
• Session cookies disappear when you close the browser; persistent cookies survive until their expiry date
• Cookies are scoped to a domain — cookies from example.com are never sent to other-site.com
• This automatic sending behavior is what makes cookies ideal for auth — no JavaScript code required
• Cookies have a size limit of about 4KB, so they typically store just an ID, not large data
• You can see your cookies in browser DevTools under Application > Cookies

cards.front: How does session-based authentication work? Walk through the full flow.
cards.back:
• Step 1: User submits username and password via a login form
• Step 2: Server verifies credentials by hashing the input and comparing to the stored hash
• Step 3: Server creates a session — an object stored on the server with a unique session ID
• Step 4: Server sends the session ID to the browser in a Set-Cookie header
• Step 5: Browser automatically includes that cookie in every future request to the server
• Step 6: On each request, the server looks up the session ID and finds the user's data
• Step 7: If the session exists and is valid, the request is treated as authenticated
• Step 8: On logout, the server deletes the session and tells the browser to clear the cookie
• The actual user data lives on the server — the browser only holds a meaningless ID string

cards.front: What are the options for storing sessions on the server? What are the tradeoffs?
cards.back:
• In-memory storage: sessions live in your server's RAM — fast but lost if the server restarts
• In-memory also fails with multiple servers because each server has its own separate memory
• Redis: an in-memory database shared across servers — fast, supports expiration, and survives restarts
• Redis is the most popular choice for production session storage because it balances speed and reliability
• Database storage (e.g., PostgreSQL): sessions are saved as rows in a table — durable but slower
• Database storage adds a DB query to every single request, which can become a bottleneck at scale
• File-based storage: sessions saved as files on disk — simple but slow and hard to share across servers
• For a single-server app, in-memory or a simple database works fine
• For production with multiple servers, Redis is the standard recommendation

cards.front: What is HTTPS and why is it critical for authentication?
cards.back:
• HTTPS encrypts all data sent between the browser and server using TLS (Transport Layer Security)
• Without HTTPS, passwords, cookies, and tokens are sent as readable text over the network
• Anyone on the same Wi-Fi network (like a coffee shop) can intercept unencrypted HTTP traffic
• This type of attack is called a man-in-the-middle attack — the attacker reads or modifies your data in transit
• HTTPS prevents this by encrypting the connection so intercepted data is useless gibberish
• The Secure flag on cookies means the browser will only send that cookie over HTTPS connections
• Modern browsers mark HTTP sites as "Not Secure" in the address bar for good reason
• Free HTTPS certificates are available from Let's Encrypt — there is no excuse to skip it
• Rule: never send any authentication data over plain HTTP, even in development if possible

cards.front: What is a JWT (JSON Web Token)? What are its three parts?
cards.back:
• A JWT is a compact, self-contained token that carries user information in a verifiable format
• It has three parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature
• The Header specifies the token type (JWT) and the signing algorithm (e.g., HS256 or RS256)
• The Payload contains claims — pieces of data like user ID, email, roles, and expiration time
• The Signature is created by signing the header and payload with a secret key
• The server can verify the signature to confirm the token was not tampered with
• Both header and payload are Base64-encoded, meaning anyone can decode and read them — they are not encrypted
• Never put sensitive data like passwords in a JWT payload because it is readable by anyone
• The key advantage: the server does not need to store anything — it just verifies the signature on each request
• You can paste any JWT into jwt.io to see its decoded contents

cards.front: What are access tokens and refresh tokens? Why do we need both?
cards.back:
• An access token is what you send with each API request to prove you are authenticated
• Access tokens are short-lived — typically 5 to 60 minutes — to limit damage if one is stolen
• A refresh token is a longer-lived token used only to get a new access token when the old one expires
• Refresh tokens typically last days or weeks and are stored more securely
• Without refresh tokens, users would have to log in again every time their short-lived access token expires
• The flow: access token expires, your app sends the refresh token to get a new access token silently
• If a refresh token is compromised, you can revoke it on the server to cut off access
• Access tokens are sent frequently (every request), so keeping them short-lived reduces the attack window
• Think of it like a day pass (access token) and a membership card (refresh token) at a gym

cards.front: Where should you store tokens in a browser? localStorage vs cookies vs memory?
cards.back:
• localStorage: easy to use from JavaScript, persists across tabs and page reloads
• localStorage risk: any JavaScript on your page can read it, so an XSS attack can steal your tokens
• HttpOnly cookies: the browser manages them and JavaScript cannot access them at all
• HttpOnly cookies are generally the safest option for storing auth tokens in a browser
• Cookies are sent automatically with requests, so you do not need to write code to attach them
• In-memory (a JavaScript variable): safest from XSS because nothing persists, but lost on page refresh
• In-memory is used in high-security apps but requires refresh tokens to restore state after reload
• Never store tokens in sessionStorage or URL parameters — both have serious security issues
• The recommended approach: store access tokens in memory or HttpOnly cookies, refresh tokens in HttpOnly cookies

cards.front: What is XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) and how does it threaten authentication?
cards.back:
• XSS is when an attacker injects malicious JavaScript into a web page that other users visit
• Example: an attacker posts a comment containing a script tag that runs on every viewer's browser
• The injected script runs with the same permissions as your legitimate JavaScript code
• It can read anything JavaScript can access: localStorage tokens, DOM content, form inputs
• An XSS attack can steal tokens from localStorage and send them to the attacker's server
• It can also make authenticated API requests on behalf of the user without them knowing
• HttpOnly cookies are immune to XSS because JavaScript cannot read them
• Prevention: always sanitize and escape user input before rendering it in HTML
• Use a framework like React or Vue — they escape output by default, which prevents most XSS
• Content Security Policy (CSP) headers add another layer of defense by restricting script sources

cards.front: What is CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) and how does it work?
cards.back:
• CSRF tricks your browser into making an unwanted request to a site where you are already logged in
• Example: you are logged into your bank, then visit a malicious site with a hidden form that posts to your bank's transfer endpoint
• Your browser automatically includes your bank's cookies with that request because that is how cookies work
• The bank's server sees valid cookies and processes the request as if you made it intentionally
• CSRF does not steal your credentials — it rides on your existing authenticated session
• Protection method 1: CSRF tokens — the server includes a random token in forms that the attacker cannot guess
• Protection method 2: SameSite cookie attribute — tells the browser not to send cookies with cross-site requests
• Protection method 3: check the Origin or Referer header to verify the request came from your own site
• CSRF mainly affects cookie-based auth because cookies are sent automatically by the browser

cards.front: What are the key cookie security attributes? (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite)
cards.back:
• Secure: the cookie is only sent over HTTPS connections, never over plain HTTP
• Without Secure, someone sniffing network traffic on HTTP can see your session cookie
• HttpOnly: JavaScript cannot access the cookie via document.cookie — only the browser sends it
• HttpOnly is your main defense against XSS attacks stealing session cookies
• SameSite=Strict: the cookie is never sent with cross-site requests — strongest CSRF protection
• SameSite=Lax: the cookie is sent with top-level navigations (clicking a link) but not with form POSTs or AJAX
• SameSite=None: the cookie is sent with all requests including cross-site — requires the Secure flag
• A good default setup: Set-Cookie: sessionId=abc; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax
• Always set all three attributes on auth cookies — there is no reason to leave them off

cards.front: What is CORS? Why do you get CORS errors during authentication?
cards.back:
• CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) controls which websites can make requests to your server
• An origin is the combination of protocol, domain, and port — http://localhost:3000 is a different origin than http://localhost:5000
• Browsers block cross-origin requests by default to prevent malicious sites from accessing your API
• During development, your frontend (localhost:3000) and backend (localhost:5000) are different origins
• The server must send Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers to tell the browser which origins are permitted
• For auth with cookies, you also need Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true on the server
• On the frontend, you must set credentials: "include" in fetch or withCredentials: true in Axios
• CORS errors only happen in browsers — they are a browser security feature, not a server issue
• The fix is always on the server: configure it to allow your frontend's origin explicitly
• Never use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials — browsers will reject it

cards.front: What is Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)? What are the common methods?
cards.back:
• MFA requires two or more different types of proof before granting access
• The three factor categories: something you know (password), something you have (phone), something you are (fingerprint)
• Even if an attacker steals your password, they still cannot log in without the second factor
• SMS codes: a one-time code texted to your phone — convenient but vulnerable to SIM-swapping attacks
• Authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy): generate time-based codes that change every 30 seconds
• Hardware keys (YubiKey): a physical USB device you plug in — the most secure option
• Email codes: a one-time code sent to your email — better than nothing but weaker than other methods
• Biometrics (fingerprint, face scan): convenient but used mainly for device unlock rather than web auth
• As a developer, you should offer MFA to users and require it for admin accounts at minimum

cards.front: What are common password attacks? (brute force, credential stuffing, phishing)
cards.back:
• Brute force: an attacker tries every possible password combination until one works
• Defense against brute force: rate limiting (e.g., max 5 login attempts per minute) and account lockout
• Dictionary attacks: a variation of brute force using lists of common passwords like "password123" or "qwerty"
• Credential stuffing: attackers use username/password pairs leaked from other breached sites
• Credential stuffing works because people reuse the same password across multiple sites
• Defense: encourage unique passwords, support MFA, and check passwords against known breach lists
• Phishing: attackers create fake login pages that look identical to real ones to trick users into entering credentials
• Defense against phishing: train users to check URLs, use MFA, and implement hardware security keys
• Rainbow table attacks: using precomputed hash lookups — defeated by salted hashing algorithms like bcrypt
• As a developer, your job is to make all of these attacks as difficult as possible through layered defenses

cards.front: What does 401 Unauthorized vs 403 Forbidden actually mean?
cards.back:
• 401 Unauthorized really means "unauthenticated" — the server does not know who you are
• You get a 401 when you send no credentials or your token is expired or invalid
• The fix for a 401 is to log in or refresh your token — you need to prove your identity
• 403 Forbidden means the server knows who you are but you do not have permission for this action
• You get a 403 when a regular user tries to access an admin-only endpoint, for example
• The fix for a 403 is not to log in again — you need a different account or elevated permissions
• The naming is confusing: 401 should have been called "Unauthenticated" but the HTTP spec used "Unauthorized"
• In your frontend code, handle these differently: 401 should redirect to login, 403 should show an access denied message
• A well-designed API uses both correctly to help clients understand what went wrong

cards.front: Sessions vs JWTs — when should you use each? What are the honest tradeoffs?
cards.back:
• Sessions: server stores user state, browser only holds a session ID in a cookie
• Sessions advantage: easy to revoke — just delete the session on the server and the user is logged out instantly
• Sessions disadvantage: the server must look up the session on every request, and you need shared storage across multiple servers
• JWTs: the token itself contains user data, the server stores nothing and just verifies the signature
• JWTs advantage: no server-side storage needed, works easily across multiple servers and microservices
• JWTs disadvantage: hard to revoke — once issued, a JWT is valid until it expires unless you build a blocklist
• Use sessions when: you have a traditional web app, need instant logout, or have a single server
• Use JWTs when: you have a microservices architecture, mobile apps, or need stateless API authentication
• Many production systems combine both: JWTs for short-lived access and server-side refresh token storage for revocation

cards.front: Auth security checklist for beginners — what is the minimum you should always do?
cards.back:
• Always use HTTPS everywhere — never send auth data over plain HTTP
• Hash passwords with bcrypt or Argon2 — never store plaintext or use fast hashes like MD5
• Set Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite attributes on all auth cookies
• Implement rate limiting on login endpoints to block brute-force attacks
• Use short-lived access tokens (15-60 minutes) and longer-lived refresh tokens
• Validate and sanitize all user input to prevent XSS and injection attacks
• Send proper CORS headers — never use wildcard origins with credentials
• Return correct HTTP status codes: 401 for unauthenticated, 403 for unauthorized
• Offer Multi-Factor Authentication, especially for admin and sensitive accounts
• Never store tokens in localStorage if you can use HttpOnly cookies instead
• Log authentication events (logins, failures, password changes) for monitoring
